# -*- sh -*-
# See http://rigaux.org/language-study/diagram.html for the format of this file
#claude: auto-generated by Claude Code (inspired from my other lineage.txt)

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Network applications and protocols lineage
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internet_protocol_suite
#src: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_the_Internet
#src: wikipedia articles on different programs

# For networking stack/libraries, see lib_networking/lineage.txt

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pre-Historical
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*ARPANET
  1969
  # first packet-switching network, BBN

*NCP
  1970
  ARPANET
  # Network Control Protocol, original ARPANET host-to-host protocol

ALOHAnet
  1971
  # University of Hawaii, wireless packet network, influenced Ethernet

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Historical (protocols)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*TCP/IP
  1974
  NCP
  # Cerf and Kahn, RFC 675 (1974), split into TCP/IP in RFC 791/793 (1981)

*Ethernet
  1973
  ALOHAnet
  SY:6
  # Xerox PARC, Bob Metcalfe
  # IEEE 802.3 standardized in 1983

*DNS
  1983
  # Paul Mockapetris, RFC 882/883, replaced hosts.txt

*SMTP
  1982
  # RFC 821, Jon Postel

*FTP
  1971
  # RFC 114, first spec; RFC 959 (1985) is the modern version

*Telnet
  1969
  ARPANET
  # RFC 15 (1969), RFC 854 (1983)

*UDP
  1980
  TCP/IP
  # RFC 768, David Reed

*SSH
  1995
  Telnet
  # Tatu Ylönen, encrypted remote shell

*DHCP
  1993
  # RFC 1531, dynamic IP assignment, replaced BOOTP

*SNMP
  1988
  # RFC 1067, network management

*BGP
  1989
  # RFC 1105, inter-domain routing, backbone of Internet routing

*OSPF
  1989
  # RFC 1131, intra-domain routing

*PPP
  1994
  # RFC 1661, point-to-point protocol

*IPsec
  1995
  # RFC 1825, IP-level encryption

*IPv6
  1998
  TCP/IP
  # RFC 2460, 128-bit addresses

*NFS
  1984
  SY:6
  # Sun Microsystems, network file system
NFS v3
  1995
NFS v4
  2000

*SSL
  1995
  SY:6
  # Netscape, SSL 3.0 (1996)

*TLS
  1999
  SSL
  SY:6
  # RFC 2246 (TLS 1.0), standardized SSL successor
TLS 1.2
  2008
*TLS 1.3
  2018
  # major simplification, 1-RTT handshake

*HTTP
  1991
  # Tim Berners-Lee, RFC 1945 (HTTP/1.0, 1996)
HTTP/1.1
  1997
  # persistent connections, chunked transfer
*HTTP/2
  2015
  # binary framing, multiplexing, derived from Google SPDY
*HTTP/3
  2022
  # QUIC-based (UDP), RFC 9114

SPDY
  2009
  HTTP/1.1
  SY:6
  # Google, precursor to HTTP/2, compressed headers, multiplexed streams

*QUIC
  2012
  UDP,TLS 1.2
  SY:6
  # Google, UDP-based transport, 0-RTT setup, connection migration
  # standardized RFC 9000 (2021)

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Historical (UNIX applications)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*inetd
  1986
  # BSD 4.3, internet super-server, listens on many ports

*sendmail
  1983
  SMTP
  # Eric Allman, dominant MTA for decades

*BIND
  1984
  DNS
  # Berkeley Internet Name Domain, dominant DNS server

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Plan9
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Plan 9 network apps
  1993
  SY:1
  # cs (connection server), dns, dhcpd, ipconfig
  # ndb (network database) instead of /etc/hosts + /etc/resolv.conf

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (web servers)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Apache httpd
  1995
  SY:2
  # "a patchy server", dominant web server for decades

*nginx
  2004
  Apache httpd
  SY:2
  # Igor Sysoev, event-driven, reverse proxy

*lighttpd
  2003
  Apache httpd
  # Jan Kneschke, lightweight event-driven web server

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (mail)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Postfix
  1998
  sendmail
  # Wietse Venema, IBM Research, secure sendmail replacement

*qmail
  1997
  sendmail
  # djb, security-focused MTA

Exim
  1995
  sendmail
  # University of Cambridge

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (DNS)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*ISC BIND 9
  2000
  BIND
  # complete rewrite

Unbound
  2007
  BIND
  # NLnet Labs, validating recursive DNS resolver

*dnsmasq
  2001
  # Simon Kelley, lightweight DNS/DHCP for small networks

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (SSH)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*OpenSSH
  1999
  SSH
  SY:2
  # Markus Friedl et al, OpenBSD, replaced rsh/rlogin/telnet

Dropbear
  2003
  SSH
  # Matt Johnston, lightweight SSH for embedded

*Mosh
  2012
  OpenSSH
  # MIT, mobile shell, handles roaming and intermittent connectivity

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Open source (tools)
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*curl
  1997
  # Daniel Stenberg, URL transfer tool/library
  # originally httpget (1996), renamed curl in 1998

*wget
  1996
  # Hrvoje Niksic, recursive download

*Wireshark
  1998
  # Gerald Combs, originally Ethereal, renamed 2006
  # packet analyzer

*tcpdump
  1988
  # Van Jacobson, packet capture tool

*nmap
  1997
  # Fyodor, network scanner

*iptables
  1998
  # Rusty Russell, Linux packet filtering (netfilter)

nftables
  2014
  iptables
  # replacement for iptables in Linux kernel

*iproute2
  1999
  # Alexey Kuznetsov, replaced ifconfig/route on Linux

*rsync
  1996
  # Andrew Tridgell, efficient file synchronization

*Samba
  1992
  # Andrew Tridgell, SMB/CIFS implementation for UNIX

*HAProxy
  2001
  # Willy Tarreau, TCP/HTTP load balancer

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Industry
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*IIS
  1995
  SY:6
  # Microsoft Internet Information Services

*Netscape Enterprise Server
  1995
  SY:6
  # later Sun ONE, then Oracle iPlanet

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Recent
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*WireGuard
  2016
  IPsec
  # Jason Donenfeld, simple VPN, merged into Linux 5.6 (2020)

*Caddy
  2015
  nginx
  # automatic HTTPS, Go-based web server

Traefik
  2015
  nginx
  # cloud-native reverse proxy, popular in Docker/Kubernetes

CoreDNS
  2016
  BIND
  # default DNS server in Kubernetes, plugin-based, Go

*Envoy
  2016
  HAProxy
  # Lyft, modern service proxy, foundation of service mesh

*Tailscale
  2019
  WireGuard
  SY:6
  # mesh VPN built on WireGuard

*Cloudflare DNS
  2018
  # 1.1.1.1, privacy-focused public DNS resolver

*DoH / DoT
  2018
  DNS,TLS
  # DNS over HTTPS (RFC 8484) / DNS over TLS (RFC 7858)

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Academic / Research
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------

*OpenFlow
  2008
  # Nick McKeown, Stanford, Software-Defined Networking
  # separated control plane from data plane, spawned an industry

P4
  2014
  OpenFlow
  # Stanford/Princeton, language for programming data planes

ns-2
  1989
  # network simulator, dominant in networking research for decades

ns-3
  2008
  ns-2
  # rewrite, not backwards-compatible, C++/Python

Mininet
  2010
  OpenFlow
  # Stanford, network emulator for SDN research
